title: Concurrent Exhibitions at List Gallery
description:
Join us for two concurrent exhibitions at the List Gallery: Paper Journeys: Pop-up Books and Constructions by Colette Fu and Views of Travel, Reverie, and Repose: Selected East Asian Works from the Swarthmore College Art Collection. Together, these exhibitions explore current and historical East Asian works and themes, such as culture and identity, travel and its transformations, and the importance of diverse narratives. Paper Journeys features mixed-media works by Colette Fu, a Philadelphia-based artist who is known for her paper constructions that explore her Nuosu Yi ancestry and the cultural traditions of diverse ethnic minority groups in China. Views of Travel, Reverie, and Repose features works from Swarthmore's College Art Collection and highlights concerns explored by East Asian artists from the 14th century to the mid-twentieth century. These interrelated exhibitions were co-curated and organized by Andrea Packard, Director of the Swarthmore College Art Collection and Curator of the List Gallery; Sara Hesdon Buehler, Art Collection Manager; and Susan Eberhard, Visiting Assistant Professor, Art History.

The exhibitions will be on view in the List Gallery from March 3 through April 19, 2026.
Colette Fu will give a talk about her work on Thursday, March 19 at 4:30 PM in the Lang Performing Arts Center Cinema; an opening reception will follow in the List Gallery from 5:30 to 7:00 PM.

title: Fetter Chamber Music Concert
description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. Dozens of student musicians were subsequently supported in their desire to explore the string quartet repertoire.

In 2001, the program was renamed the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and was expanded to support the coaching of multiple chamber music groups. Funding continues to be enhanced by successive generations of the Fetter family, including Bob Fetter ’53, Tom Fetter ’56, Ellen Fetter, and Annie Fetter ’88. Swarthmore College, students and faculty past and present, and the community at large, has been enriched by the musical communication made possible through the generosity of the Fetter family.

This performance is free and open to the public.  Seating is first come first served. This performance will be streamed live.

title: Environmental Studies Program Colloquium Series: Kyle Whyte
description:
Environmental Studies Program 2026 Colloquium Series

Talk by: Kyle Whyte     

Date:  Monday, April 20th, 2026
 
Time:  12:00 pm - 1:00 pm  (Bring your lunch)

 
Location:  SCI Cunniff (SC 199)

Title: "Why Does Anything Need to be Called Wild?" **



The impacts of climate change and biodiversity loss are creating extreme unease about the future of natural places. Moreover, groups such as Indigenous peoples are facing some of the most acute harms. Yet, is wilderness the best term to call these places? This presentation will discuss why Indigenous peoples have alternative ways to conceptualize climate mitigation and biodiversity conservation that need not lean on the use of the term "wilderness." 
 

** Kyle Whyte, "Why Does Anything Need to be Called Wild?", In, The Heart of the Wild: Essays on Nature, Conservation, and the Human Future, edited by Ben A. Minteer and Jonathan B. Loso (Princeton University Press, 2024).

 


 

 


Kyle Whyte is the George Willis Pack Professor in the School for Environment and Sustainability and University Diversity and a Social Transformation Professor. He teaches in and coordinates the School’s environmental justice graduate specialization. He is founding Faculty Director of the Tishman Center for Social Justice and the Environment, Principal Investigator of the Environmental Justice + Humanities Hub, co-Principal Investigator of the Global Center for Climate Change and Transboundary Waters, Faculty Associate of Native American Studies, Principal Investigator of the Secretariat for the Pathways Alliance for Change and Transformation, STRIDE Committee member, affiliate Professor of Philosophy, and Senior Fellow in the Michigan Society of Fellows. Whyte’s research on environmental justice centers on moral and political issues that Indigenous peoples are addressing in the areas of climate change, conservation, and cooperative relationships with science institutions. He is an enrolled member of the Citizen Potawatomi Nation. His publications appear in journals including Climatic Change, Weather, Climate & Society, Science, Daedalus, WIREs Climate Change, Environment & Planning E, and Sustainability Science.


 


Co-sponsored by the Environmental Justice & Community Resilience Program at the Lang Center, Environmental Studies Program, Office of Sustainability and the Mellon Foundation.

title: Dr. Kyle Whyte Lecture/ “How Does Indigenous Peoples' Climate Action Scale Up?”
description: Indigenous peoples’ actions to address climate change continue to achieve meaningful effects, impacting the United Nations, multinational industries, regional governance, and national laws, policies, and investments. Yet many people assume that only multilateral institutions and nations can move the needle at global and regional scales. Indigenous peoples are often labeled as actors who can make change slowly and at local scales. The assumption that Indigenous actions cannot achieve results 'at scale' is wrong. Indigenous movements show how responsible relationships, kinship bonds, care for land, and self-determination are effective elements of methods for scaling up. Compared to dominant methods of scaling up, which are replete with tradeoffs that trend toward worsening climate risk, carbon footprints, and human suffering, Indigenous methods are ethical, just, and can be organized to achieve speed.

title: SwatTalk: Alumni Author Jamie Madden ’06
description:
SwatTalk: Jamie Madden ’06

Monday, April 20, 2026 at 8:00 p.m.

RSVP

Join Jamie Madden ’06 in conversation with moderator Twan Claiborne ’07 about his recently published memoir Bittersweet Lane: Creating Home(s) in the American Affordable Housing Crisis. We’ll discuss the nature of the housing crisis, what is necessary to solve it, and the role of lived experience in the professional and academic worlds.

Jamie Madden ’06 wrote Bittersweet Lane: Creating Home(s) in the American Affordable Housing Crisis to demystify affordable housing while exploring the meaning of home from an intergenerational, lower-class perspective. Jamie grew up in affordable housing at the Bittersweet Lane Apartments and went on to work for the affordable housing industry’s leading nonprofits. He is a dad, a community development professional, and a Truman Scholar. He holds degrees from MIT and Swarthmore College. Jamie's work has helped create more than 1,000 affordable homes.

SwatTalks is an Alumni Council initiative that engages the broader Swarthmore community through seminars featuring alumni and faculty members excelling in their fields. Each hour-long online seminar is free and open to Swarthmore alumni, families, faculty, staff, students, and friends. We welcome suggestions for future speakers and topics — please email your ideas to alumni@swarthmore.edu.

title: The Harvard School of Meaning- Centered Medical Anthropology: Reflections After 50 Years
description:
The Harvard School of Meaning-Centered Medical Anthropology: Reflections After 50 Years


 

WHEN: Tuesday, April 21, 2026, 4:30 pm

WHERE: Science Center 101

 

Why should meaning-centered Medical Anthropology matter for undergraduates, social scientists, and educated people globally? This talk uses the story of Harvard's five decades of medical anthropological teaching, research and practice to illustrate why a meaning-centered approach to experience is so vital for our times. That approach creates a framework for the practical application of wisdom derived from the human sciences and puts it to work to reimagine our social condition and develop more availing approaches and practices. This talk is dedicated to a former student and leader of the Harvard program: Paul Farmer, whose brilliance and moral passion informed our emphasis on accompaniment, human rights, and integration of the moral, the emotional and the practical.

description:
“What’s in a (Building’s) Name?”


Scheuer Room, Kohlberg Hall
April 22, 4:30-6:00 p.m.

Panelists:

Lisa Alvarez-Cohen, Vice Provost for Academic Planning, UC Berkeley
Erin Brownlee Dell, Chief of Staff and Secretary of the College, Swarthmore College
Kimberly Wright Cassidy ’85, H’25, Ninth President, Bryn Mawr College
Roberta “Bobbi” Cordano, President, Gallaudet University

Catherine "Cat" Norris, Associate Dean of the Faculty for Academic Programs and Research, Swarthmore College 

Description:

This is the third in a series of events on the renaming of Old Science Hall. 

Lisa Alvarez-Cohen, an environmental engineer whose research focuses on the application of microbiological processes for the elimination of environmental contaminants, was named Berkeley’s vice provost for academic planning in 2018. Last year, Berkeley chose an Indigenous name for a campus building and consulted with Indigenous people in the process.

Kimberly Wright Cassidy ’85, H’25, joined Bryn Mawr’s psychology faculty in 1993 and served as its ninth president from 2013-2024. During her tenure as president, she oversaw the renaming of Bryn Mawr’s library.  

Cat Norris is Associate Dean of the Faculty for Academic Programs and Research and an associate professor of psychology. She joined Swarthmore’s faculty in 2013.

title: Journal of World Literature Event: Panel discussion on the Role of Empathy in Literature
description:
Join Susan Muaddi Darraj, Arnab Dutta Roy, and Shailen Mishra for a discussion on how narrative empathy can serve as a conceptual framework for thinking ethically about otherness and for Global South solidarities against colonialism and genocide. The event will also include a talk and reading by Prof. Muaddi Darraj, and promotion of the upcoming book Constructing the Other: Narrative Empathy and the Ethics of Border-Crossing in World Literature by Profs. Dutta Roy and Mishra. 

 

Attendees can join the event virtually as well. Please follow the link to register: (link)

 

Susan Muaddi Darraj is the author of the novel Behind You Is The Sea, which received praise from The New York Times, The San Francisco Chronicle, and Ms Magazine; it was also named a Best Book of 2024 by The New Yorker. Her other major works are the short story collection, A Curious Land, and the Farah Rocks children’s book series. She teaches creative writing at The Johns Hopkins University in Baltimore.


Arnab Dutta Roy is Assistant Professor of English at Florida Gulf Coast University. His research is located at the intersection of postcolonialism, human rights, and modern South Asian literature. He has co-edited two books on the postcolonial bildungsroman and a special issue on narrative empathy and world literature in the Journal of World Literature.

Shailen Mishra is a Visiting Assistant Professor at Swarthmore College. His research interests include writing studies, creative writing aesthetics, and narrative empathy. He has co-edited a special issue for the Journal of World Literature and his creative writing has appeared in DIAGRAM, The Baltimore Review, The Bookends Review, and elsewhere.   

This scholarly promotion event is co-sponsored by the Aydelotte Foundation, Swarthmore College Libraries, and the Department of Peace and Conflict Studies.  


This event is free and to the public and the Tri-Co community.

title: Narrating the Goddess: Professor Jessica Birkenholtz's Translation on Swasthani Vrata Kartha
description: The Swasthani Vrata Katha is Nepal’s most widely read and recited Hindu devotional text. Dating to the late 16th century, many Nepali Hindus annually perform a month-long recitation of it still today. It is a collection of widely circulating Hindu myths about gods, demons, and divine dalliances that forefront the great god Shiva and the goddesses Sati and Parvati. It is also a local folk story about women’s real, everyday hardships and the rewards of attending to one’s dharma and devotion to the goddess Swasthani. In this talk, Professor Birkenholtz draws on her decades-long study of the Swasthani to reflect, first, on how the Swasthani both prescribes idealized womanhood for Nepali women and illustrates many lived realities. Second, she will consider how the portrayal and treatment of women is variously experienced as misogynistic or empowering among Nepali women. Finally, Professor Birkenholtz will share her experience as a feminist scholar of religion of translating the Swasthani into English with a female Nepali scholar and collaborator.

description:
An unforgettable story, told on one of New York's greatest stages.

Swarthmore Night at Lincoln Center Theater: Ragtime

6:15 p.m.

Lincoln Center Theater
150 W 65th Street
New York
$69 per person

This event has reached capacity. To be added to a waitlist, please contact alumni@swarthmore.edu.

We’re coming together to experience Ragtime, a breathtaking musical adaptation of E.L. Doctorow’s celebrated novel. The story beautifully weaves together the lives of three fictional families — a Black pianist and his beloved; a Jewish immigrant and his daughter; and a wealthy white family — all in pursuit of the American Dream at the dawn of the 20th century.

Space for this event is extremely limited, and we expect it to fill up quickly. If you're thinking about joining us, don't wait — reserve your discounted loge ticket today.

6:30 p.m. — Mix, Mingle, & Learn: We'll have a special opportunity to hear from Jenna Clark Embrey, LCT's Literary Manager and Dramaturg, in conversation with Kathy Liu ’04, LCT’s Director of Development.

title: Senior Lang Scholar Presentations
description:
Launched in the early 80s at Swarthmore College by Eugene M. Lang ’38, the Lang Opportunity Scholarship Program selects up to six members of the sophomore class each year and provides them with an array of undergraduate and graduate benefits – including a $12k project grant.

To date, LOS has cultivated 100s of Lang Scholars who have partnered with 100s more communities on projects all over the world. Join the LOS Class of 2026 to hear about their inspiring projects! They are:


  • Mya Chuluundorj's project, “Story-Capturers: The Voices of the Land,” is a digital archive preserving the cultural heritage of Mongolian rural communities, engaging students against climate change and urbanization.  
  • Lena Habtu created a documentary with Human Rights First Ethiopia highlighting Tigrayans’ experiences during the 2020-2022 conflict.
  • Prerna Karmacharya’s project seeks to decrease the underrepresentation of blind and visually impaired individuals in STEM fields like robotics in partnership with Perkins School for the Blind. 
  • Steven Mukum’s project, Rising Above Water, addresses internal displacement in Dangbo, Benin, caused by climate-related flooding through training in sustainable agriculture and providing flood-resistant infrastructure in partnership with OJECC Bénin.
  • Billy Wu’s project, Acquired Tomorrows, reduces stigma and increases access to mental health with project partner, Home for the Disabled Kunshan and ShangXiang Counseling.

title: Senior Case 2026
description:
The Senior Showcase provides a space where the Swarthmore community comes together to learn from senior projects across all three divisions. Participating students each have three minutes and one slide to present their work. Why only three minutes when our students could spend hours sharing the details of their projects? Most people will not take more than three minutes to listen and understand a student's work; therefore, students need to learn both how to distill their work into a three-minute sound bite and how to explain their work to a more general audience. Three minutes also allows for more participation from interested seniors.

title: DRAGMORE
description:
Step into DRAGMORE - a kaleidoscope of fabulousness where every sequin shimmers with stories untold and every strut is a declaration of fierce empowerment. Our drag show isn't just a spectacle; it's a journey into a world where glitter reigns supreme and authenticity knows no bounds.

Get ready to be dazzled as performers take the stage, transforming into larger-than-life personas with a wink, a nod, and a whole lot of attitude. From jaw-dropping lip-syncs to gravity-defying dance moves, each act is a celebration of self-expression and uninhibited creativity.

As the lights dim and the music pulses through your veins, prepare to be swept away on a whirlwind of glamour, humor, and unapologetic fabulousness. Whether you're a seasoned drag aficionado or a first-time reveler, our show promises an unforgettable evening of laughter, love, and pure, unadulterated fun. So grab your glitter, unleash your inner diva, and join us for a night where anything is possible—and everyone is a star.

title: Fetter Chamber Music Concert
description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. Dozens of student musicians were subsequently supported in their desire to explore the string quartet repertoire.

In 2001, the program was renamed the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and was expanded to support the coaching of multiple chamber music groups. Funding continues to be enhanced by successive generations of the Fetter family, including Bob Fetter ’53, Tom Fetter ’56, Ellen Fetter, and Annie Fetter ’88. Swarthmore College, students and faculty past and present, and the community at large, has been enriched by the musical communication made possible through the generosity of the Fetter family.

This performance is free and open to the public.  Seating is first come first served. This performance will be streamed live.

description: The Arabic Language Table provides a relaxed, supportive space for students to practice conversational Arabic, build confidence, and engage with the language beyond the classroom. title: Lenape: Resilience, Return, and Renewal
description:
Jeremy Johnson, Cultural Education Director, Delaware Tribe of Indians (Lenape)


Scheuer Room, Kohlberg Hall

April 29, 4:30-6:00 p.m.

Description:

This is the fourth and final event in a series on the renaming of Old Science Hall.

In this talk, Johnson examines the deep history of the Lenape people in their ancestral homelands and the profound impacts of displacement through colonial expansion and forced removal. It traces how, despite these disruptions, the Lenape have sustained cultural continuity through language, traditions, and the transmission of knowledge across generations.The presentation highlights ongoing efforts to return - physically, culturally, and spiritually – to Lënapehokink, emphasizing initiatives that restore presence, strengthen community ties, and renew relationships to land. Centering both historical realities and contemporary leadership, it underscores how resilience and acts of return continue to shape Lenape identity and future pathways grounded in sovereignty, connection, and renewal.

Jeremy Johnson is the cultural education director of the Delaware Tribe of Indians, based in Bartlesville, Okla. He is Lenape, Absentee Shawnee, and Peoria. Prior to his current role, he served as assistant chief of the Delaware Tribe. A lifelong educator, he is committed to preserving and revitalizing Lenape culture and language for the future generations of his tribe. 

All events are sponsored by the Swarthmore College President’s Office.

title: Fetter Chamber Music Concert
description:
The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and the Department of Music present student musicians from the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Program in a series of chamber music concerts. 

The Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program has been an integral part of the Swarthmore College performance program for four decades. It began in 1975 as the ""Pollard Scholarship Funds,"" with an initial contribution from Elizabeth Pollard Fetter '25 in memory of her mother, Emilie Garrett Pollard '93. With the passing of Elizabeth Pollard Fetter in 1977, husband Frank Whitson Fetter '20 stepped forward to endow the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter String Quartet Scholarships at Swarthmore College. Dozens of student musicians were subsequently supported in their desire to explore the string quartet repertoire.

In 2001, the program was renamed the Elizabeth Pollard Fetter Chamber Music Program and was expanded to support the coaching of multiple chamber music groups. Funding continues to be enhanced by successive generations of the Fetter family, including Bob Fetter ’53, Tom Fetter ’56, Ellen Fetter, and Annie Fetter ’88. Swarthmore College, students and faculty past and present, and the community at large, has been enriched by the musical communication made possible through the generosity of the Fetter family.

This performance is free and open to the public.  Seating is first come first served. This performance will be streamed live.

title: Senior Honors Thesis Staged Reading
description:
Spring 2026 Honors Playwriting Thesis 

The Night of Ended Secrets by Frank Paz (‘26)

Directed by Andrew Watring

Jamie and Carmine, two estranged childhood friends, reconnect as they sit vigil for the death of Jamie’s grandmother. As Jamie struggles to write his eulogy, he must choose: keep a secret out of love for his grandmother or risk living his authentic truth. Written by Frank Paz (‘26), The Night of Ended Secrets pits familial obligation against burning desire against the setting of one family’s grief.  

Performance at 8:00 p.m.

title: SwatWalks: Oakland
description:
Step outside and connect with your local community through SwatWalks, our new alumni-led series of outdoor social strolls! We are kicking off our very first outing in the Bay Area with a walk around Oakland's Lake Merritt.

Join host Margaret Huang '87 and fellow alumni for our inaugural SwatWalks: Oakland — a casual, family-friendly walk around scenic Lake Merritt, the nation's first wildlife refuge.

Join this easy 3-mile walk, which is perfect for all ages and abilities. Whether you’re seeking fresh air, lively conversation, or just a great reason to get outside, this is your morning. Afterward, join us for an optional brunch and music at Lake Chalet. Bring your sunscreen and your Swat spirit — let's walk, talk, and enjoy the spring.

title: Peter van de Kamp Observatory Open House
description:
The Physics and Astronomy department hosts a telescope open house on the second Tuesday of each month at the Peter van de Kamp Observatory on the roof of the Science Center. Directions are available at www.swarthmore.edu/peter-van-de-kamp-observatory/directions.

The open house hours are 8:00 p.m. to 9:00 p.m. during standard time and 9:00 p.m. to 10:00 p.m. during daylight savings time.

If the weather is bad, the open house will be cancelled and an announcement will be posted on the Physics and Astronomy department website and the College calendar. Check https://www.swarthmore.edu/physics-astronomy on the day of the open house if the weather is questionable

Open to the public.

title: Garnet Day
description:
Welcome the long-awaited springtime with music and dance! 

This Garnet Day, stop by the beautiful new Arts Plaza behind Martin Hall between 10:30 a.m. and 12:30 p.m. to enjoy musical and dance performances from our talented student artists. There will also be treats from The Chilly Banana food truck (vegan options available) and the chance to talk to folks from Get Out the Vote, the Office of Sustainability, the Swarthmore Coalition on Well-Being, Student Disability Services, and the Inclusive Excellence Fellows Initiatives.

Garnet Day Springtime Music and Dance Celebration

Arts Plaza, Behind Martin Hall

Thursday, April 2

10:30 a.m. – 12:30 p.m.

Rain location Lang Concert Hall and Lobby

We hope you can join us in celebrating the warmer weather and our talented community members. As always, remember to wear your Swarthmore gear to show your spirit!

Congratulations to last month's Garnet Day Guessing Game winners: Charlotte Dustin ‘29 (first place), Kat Matekovic (second place), and Amanda Whitbred, Communications (third place)! There were 1745 pieces of candy in the jar.
location: Parrish East Parlors - Piano, Parrish Lawn - Beach, Parrish West Parlors, Front Lawn of Pearson, Old Science Hall Lawn, Foulk Walk, Singer First Floor Commons, Singer Terrace

title: Peter van de Kamp Observatory Open House
description:
The Physics and Astronomy department hosts a telescope open house on the second Tuesday of each month at the Peter van de Kamp Observatory on the roof of the Science Center. Directions are available at www.swarthmore.edu/peter-van-de-kamp-observatory/directions.

The open house hours are 8:00 p.m. to 9:00 p.m. during standard time and 9:00 p.m. to 10:00 p.m. during daylight savings time.

If the weather is bad, the open house will be cancelled and an announcement will be posted on the Physics and Astronomy department website and the College calendar. Check https://www.swarthmore.edu/physics-astronomy on the day of the open house if the weather is questionable

Open to the public.
location: Science Center Peter Van de Kamp Observatory, Science Center Observatory Computer Room
